video_title: APPLE VISION PRO È STATO UN FALLIMENTO?
comment: Grazie per questo video, ed è una partenza per tanti spunti. Ad oggi credo che come brand per poter continuare a crescere, ha necessità di entrare in campi dove non sarà l'unico player e quindi risentirà della concorrenza! La mia impressione è che: ad oggi, è in balia del mercato, ma che comunque non risponda a bisogni reali. Non individuando un preciso target di mercato, non è possibile sviluppare in modo giusto a mio parere! Ad esempio in ambito chirurgico, che il mio mondo avrei piacere di vedere implementazioni che giustificherebbero i 3000 euro ed anzi sarebbero irrisori. Questa sarebbe ricerca, che darebbe un senso ed il tempo di creare tecnologie più popolari! Mi piace il punto di vista tuo e di Otto Climan e credo che sia l'hardware di base giusto che ovviamente si dovrà necessariamente avvicinare all'occhiale,  in termini di comodità e funzionalità, abbandonando un po' il mondo delle applicazioni e avvicinandosi di più all' intelligenza artificiale. Credo che il dispositivo ideale lo conosciamo già grazie al mondo Marvel con i fumetti e film di Iron Man. Ma è ovvio che non possiamo ancora arrivarci! Da qui si aprono tanti altri discorsi...

video_title: APPLE VISION PRO È STATO UN FALLIMENTO?
comment: Che questa storia possa riportare molti tech influencer con i piedi per terra. Quando è uscito questo Vision Pro sembrava chissà che cosa, tutti ad osannarlo e a descriverlo come una mega rivoluzione, quando in realtà è un prodotto che non è né carne né pesce. Realisticamente parlando, la più grande fetta di persone che ha necessità di un visore è rappresentata dai gamers, poi ci sono le nicchie delle nicchie che lo possono usare sul lavoro. E questo Vision Pro non è utilizzabile né dai gamers (libreria di giochi pressoché inesistente) né da quelle nicchie di lavoratori (applicazioni diffuse nel mercato del lavoro pressoché inesistenti).

É molto interessante, sicuramente una rivoluzione dei visori molto importante; ma 3500€ di visore e 2500€ di pc apple... 
Non mi sembra molto abbordabile per le persone comuni, per una azienda potrebbe tornare molto utile, basta pensare a chi fa le installazioni come l'elettricista o il meccanico, ma una persona qualunque che non dispone di grandi somme di denaro non se lo puó permettere; bello intuitivo semplice da usare... adatto a tutti solo se hai i soldi.
Ribadisco che é molto interessante, bello e figo, ma assolutamente non indispensabile per la quotidianità.

video_title: TUTTA LA VERITÀ SU APPLE VISION PRO.
comment: La questione principale è che l'hardware non è adeguato alla visione. Fino a quando l'hardware sarà un elemento di disturbo (peso, senso di  costrizione, ecc ecc) rimarrà un oggetto che esalta ma poi finisce in un angolo. La cosa che più mi preoccupa, da persona che ha qualche anno in piò, è che mi sembra comunque che crei una distanza percettive tra le persone, immaginiamoci di vedere un film in compagnia con sto coso addosso. Continuo a credere che il futuro degli hardware sia su occhiali AR tipo XREAL o simili, che però per ora hanno sistemi operativi ed interfacce ridicoli. Per ora mi sembra solo uno strumento per sviluppatori per le future generazioni di visori, quando saranno meno invasivi e confortevoli e magari meno costosi.

video_title: TUTTA LA VERITÀ SU APPLE VISION PRO.
comment:
gli antichi già avevano risolto il problema del peso sulla testa, non per gli elmi da guerra ma per le corone reali, pesantissime e scomode. Il peso doveva essere distribuito su tutta la circonferenza, mettendo pietre e massa aurea anche nei punti non visibili, se avessero adottato una conformazione a diadema avrebbe causato problemi al collo , per questo quelli femminili sono sottilissimi ed arzigogolati, per creare vuoti di metallo e nel contempo ospitare molte pietre solo nella parte frontale.
Apple avrebbe dovuto integrare il laccio unico elastico con componenti elettroniche su tutta la circonferenza lasciando sul frontale solo il peso strettamente necessario, bilanciando i pesi.

comment: Fun fact... "Foveated Rendering" was also used in the Royal Air Force's Harrier GR3 Flight Simulator back in the late 1980s, the pilot wore a special helmet that tracked the movement of the eyes so that the projection on to the screen only had to render in detail where the pilot was looking as of course back then computing power, especially for video rendering, was extremely limited.

video_title: Apple Vision Pro Review: Tomorrow's Ideas... Today's Tech!
comment:
Thank you for this demonstration. It was very well done. What I have been looking to see is how I can zoom in and out. I am legally blind and use the Zoom feature on my iMac and MacBook. I really would like to see if/how the Zoom feature can help me read text within the windows and also text on a wall in the room or signage such as the name on a store or the text on a street sign. Please consider demonstrating this feature if it exists on the Apple Vision Pro.

On iMac and MacBook, the Zoom feature I use can be turned on as follows:

Settings >> Accessibility >> Zoom >> Toggle On "Use scroll gesture with modifier keys to zoom" >> Leave "Modifier key for scroll gesture" set to the default "Control" key >> Set Zoom style to "Full Screen".

Obviously, if I could use this feature in the Apple Vision Pro, I could have full access to the environment around me. So please, if you could, I know for a fact there are thousands of people who would greatly appreciate the demonstration.

video_title: Apple Vision Pro Unboxing!
comment: 5:15 the weight is dependent on the number of battery cells, that are used. The number of battery cells is dependent on the voltage used. You can make a 3166 mAh battery at 3,7 V or you can make the a battery with the same nominal capacity with 7,4 V, but then you need double the amount of cells (double the weight). That is why often people use Wh instead of mAh, then the "capacity" of the battery is independent from its voltage.

video_title: Apple Vision Pro Hidden Feature ✈️ #protip
comment:
Apple Vision Pro employs a tracking mode commonly referred to as "world tracking" in computer vision (visual odometry). It continuously generates a map as you move, but the background must remain static for it to function effectively. Our team is currently developing this mode for our webAR SDK.

However, there is a slight correction to be made, Marques. When you switch to travel mode, it deactivates world tracking, not the gyroscope. What you are experiencing is commonly known as Gyro Mode tracking. When you are moving, your AR essentially stays with you, and it utilizes accelerometer data as well for fine-tuning.

comment:
I’ve seen quite a few people comment on multiple Apple vision/VR sets about how we used to tell kids to not sit too close to the TV but now we put screens on our eye balls.
Well, I have an actual response from an eye doctor and a mildly good reason for my son to sit close to the TV.
My son had/has exotropia in one eye meaning his eye looks out and up, it’s like the opposite of a lazy eye. To fix this they gave him glasses to direct the eye forward and a patch to beef up the muscles. 
Sitting super close to the TV actually helped that eye focus straight and forward. The eye doctor said if he had a lazy eye and it had turned towards his nose, that sitting too close to the tv wouldn’t help at all. 
And he said, kids tend to enjoy being fully immersed in the TV. No joke, so do adults apparently and thus we have, VR headsets. 
Thank you for reading. 😂

video_title: Apple Vision Pro is Missing Something...
comment:
As someone who works professionally with AR/VR/MR (collectively XR) headsets, I’m so glad you are doing these reviews of the Vision Pro (saves me a lot of work 😂). 

The first concept you describe, the ability to share virtual objects with someone else who is physically in the same room as you, is called World Anchoring, or more specifically sharing world anchors between the two headsets. Essentially both headsets agree on a common world anchor, and place/update all virtual objects accordingly. This is already a thing in the Quest Pro and Quest 3, as well as other higher end headsets like the HoloLens 2 and Magic Leap 2 (similarly priced around $3500-$5000). I’m surprised (but not really) that Apple isn’t on board with this concept right out of the box. 

The next concept, sharing a virtual environment with someone else who’s not co-located with you, just as you said is very straight forward. 

As for remembering unique spaces on the headset, HoloLens came up with a janky-but-effective solution for this years ago (like circa 2016 HoloLens 1 timeframe): HoloLens builds up a spatial map of the room/environment, and remembers the name of the wifi network that it was connected to when it built that map. It then saves that map to its internal memory. The next time the headset connects to the same WiFi network, it recognizes the name, and immediately loads up the pre-built spatial map for that area. This saves on processing power by not having to recreate the spatial map every time the headsets boots up.  HoloLens can remember multiple WiFi signals/spatial maps (they’re on the order of MB of data, so not much compared to its 64GB of onboard memory. 

I’m sure the list could go on. Again, surprised-but-not-surprised that Apple hasn’t figured any of this out yet. 😂

comment: Have had mine since they released and I still absolutely love it! I use it every day and ended up getting rid of my iPad bc it was just collecting dust after a couple of months. The Vision 2.0 improvements are absolutely awesome! The gesture for home and control center are crucial now bc i almost never use the crown anymore and the experience is all the better for it. The 3D photos are insanely cool and fun to go thru old photos of my son and family and pop them out in 3D! The other subtle improvements are just making this better and better. But as you say, lack of apps, the devs who wont release a compatible app and lack of full immersive content is a drag, but at least more immersive videos are on the way as soon as next week. They need some more quality and native apps tho for sure, but the device is still only a few months old. I remember the first iPhone and it took a very long while for quality apps to start coming along. I think we’ll get there in time. When they do release a better, lighter headset in the future, I’ll be all for that too and hopefully it’ll be far more affordable. comment: Great video. People have either purposely misled others or been genuinely ignorant about why this AVP was created and released. The AVP was NEVER expected to be a mass consumption device. Apple released their intention to create only 400k units. They produced 15 million AirPods, 10-15 mil watches year 1. Apple described it as the future of personal computing, not the present. Apple HAD to finally release the device to show developers and corporations Apple’s API for VR. They HAD to open it up to the content creators to start developing content. Very one says this is an expensive device but compared to PCVR headsets with the same displays it’s great value. The upcoming Pimax Crystal Super is approx £2500(with taxes) and then you still need a pc powerful enough to power it so add another £3000+. Other Pc headsets with similar displays are going to be closer to 10k. Yes compared to a Quest 3 it costs more but the capability’s are vastly different. Tech journalists need to change the story about price and compare it to comparable devices.

I probably have over £3000 of monitors on the desk and the Vision Pro is incredibly close to removing the need for them(then it’s break even cost wise) but on the road and working remote it’s incredible and worth very penny.

When these devices cost closer to £1000 I think they will get more mass market uptake and if they can get the tech into something closer to a glasses that would be a game changer.

Sdk wise it has the best one on the market for writing “business” and productivity apps. Microsoft used to have a great VR SDK but they are stopping support for it.

video_title: How Vision Pro Can BOOST Your Productivity!
comment:
foveated rendering uses hardware eye tracking in combination with software to allocate GPU power to wherever you’re looking on the display and make it as high fidelity as possible, and then everything else outside of your center vision is lower fidelity. this circle of clarity follows wherever you are looking on the display.

The reason why the edges of the DISPLAY are not very sharp has nothing to do with foveated rendering. it’s simply a hardware limitation due to lensing and probably other physical factors that no one has figured out yet how to overcome in this current form factor.

video_title: How Vision Pro Can BOOST Your Productivity!
comment:
I think you are likely confusing foveated rendering with just the fact that things that are near the edges of the field of view are blurrier because of the optics.
Foveated rendering is based on where you eyes are looking - so if you are looking down at the bottom of a document, it is rendering that as high quality as it can. But if then moving you head to "recenter" it makes it sharper - that is an optics limitation, not foveated rendering. While the end result is the same, foveated rendering shouldn't get the blame for that :)

video_title: Apple Vision Pro Unboxing - Are We There Yet?
comment:
I bought and still have the Apple Vision Pro And no I’m not rich, I just worked hard and saved up for six months. The Apple Vision Pro is an amazing piece of technology! A piece of technology that you can’t just look at and understand how amazing it is. if you can, go to your nearest Apple store and try it out. Not to just buy it but to see the future of tech. 

Extra info:
-The Apple Vision Pro battery size is closer to 10,000 mah
-The Apple Vision Pro has two desktop level chips and a headphone chip built into it, the quest has one.  The quest can run three apps simultaneously. The Apple Vision Pro, how big is your house? all in 4K. Not the same league
-If you were to move the battery or tap it, it actually gives you a very simplistic display of where your battery is with its charge via a light
-Apple is coming at this device as a consumption device, no doubt. The environments, immersive video, all lead into that ideal. After all, it pays to entertain people. 
-It’s also an amazing research device with as many panels as you want floating around you answering your questions

We got one for work and someone logged on with their personal account, it synced all there photos and I could not log them off.  Finally when the came back from holiday they logged off, but there photos were all synced to the AVP.
Nothing too dodgy on there, but worrying on a shared headset.
I need to look into the guest mode, But don’t want to lock it down for anyone.
I guess there is compromise to how we plan to use it in work.
One main User and guests, or open access.

 It is better than quest visual quality, but the quest link to steam, and pc is more useful than locked in apple apps.
Hopefully more apps for pc linking will come out in July.

video_title: Does visionOS 2 Make Vision Pro WORTH It?!
comment:
- I can imagine air traffic controllers and the military will find the Apple Vision Pro useful to show many screens simultaneously that are 150-400 inches each...that's a lot of information.
- Current F16 figher helmets are $400k each with just a HUD, whereas the AVP could be incorporated into a helmet for far less than $400k.
- Infantry soldiers wearing a helmet with the AVP.
- AVP's passthrough is 120 degrees which allow real world interaction with the firearm, target, throwing/catching.
- I want to get the Apple Vision Pro, it's not that expensive compared to a great home theater, and people don't realize that it's got eye tracking (no mouse cursor), an 8k screen, many many 300 in screens, 3D camera, spatial audio speakers, creates an Imax or the biggest commercial theater screen size even when you're sitting in a tight coach airline seat.
- the eye tracking (replaced a cursor) us a game changer.
- way easier to develop than Facebook Quest.
- AVP is not constrained to a box game model like the Quest, instead the AVP world model is infinite.
- air traffic controllers can see the perspective of a commercial plane pilot landing.
- The AVP's many many virtual 150in-300in screens can replace a commercial airline cockpit's many physical screens, and that physical space can hold more important physical equipments.

Top of my wishlist would be an alternative selection gesture — an alternative to the thumb-index-finger pinch.  Not suggesting that the pinch is difficult to do, but exactly the opposite:  It’s too easy to do unintentionally/unknowingly do!  

I’ve seen a lot of cases where I’m trying like crazy to select something, and it just won’t select.  Why?  Because, unbeknownst to me, my _other hand_ has fallen into my lap with my forefinger touching my thumb!  Yes, I know we can limit pinches to one hand, but if you do so, you can no longer size things with a two-handed pinch.  Plus, I still sometimes inadvertently pinch with the one hand.

An alternative gesture thumb-to-second finger would be very helpful.

video_title: Apple Vision Pro visionOS: Hands On with the UI, Apps, & More!
comment:
Instead of only viewing a window upright in front of us, I'm hoping we can view windows and other digital elements on a flat surface like a desk - like paper. Imagine viewing a multi-page PDF on your desk like a real paper document. You could make it larger than a real paper document, zoom in, and scroll or flip through the pages like a real document. Maybe even have a slight shadow underneath it depending on the light in your environment. Imagine designing a poster using Adobe Illustrator or Photoshop with the Apple Pencil becoming a real art tool like a paint brush. You could work on your art either upright or flat on a desk or floor. You could move the Adobe panels/palettes around your desk and they would be three-dimensional like real art tools. At this point, viewing everything inside a floating window just seems a bit uninspired. It's cool, but I think we want dimensional objects we can "hold." Also, imagine a sculpture app that allows you to create virtual pottery on a virtual pottery wheel with your hands, or use your ApplePencil as a chisel to carve a life-sized sculpture of Michelangelo's David from a virtual block of marble.

I don't know how I feel about the apps being circular instead of the usual squares with rounded corners. If they kept them as squares, it would tie in with the other OSs for consistency. The circles crop too much into the app's art. It works well for Safari and Photos because the icons are circular. But it feels tight when you see the multiple apps grouped into that one circle. They might be trying to copy the app icons on the AppleWatch. I'm excited to see where this goes.

video_title: Experience Spatial Computing NOW With Xreal Air AR Glasses!
comment:
As a skeptic of the technology, I first purchased a pair of cheaper TCL glasses (on sale )about a year ago to test drive a version of this type of tech. Long story short, the tech is impressive but a novelty, especially with the TCL glasses, as they are heavier, uncomfortable, and did not have any way to adjust brightness on-device. With the pending release of XReal Beam, I decided to give the XReal Airs a shot. Out of the box, no Nebula software at all, these glasses stand head and shoulders above my older TCL glasses, almost reinventing the experience altogether. The Airs are unexpectedly light (even if you expect them to be light) and extremely comfortable to wear for long periods. Content really pops and looks amazing on these things, giving you a new and innovative way to experience what otherwise might be same-old, same-old. Out of the box, this feels like the best pair of glasses you can get for a personal display. 

The Nebula app—the beta software unlocking spatial computing capability—is a fun curiosity at this point, working reasonably well for what it is, but has its own limitations that the pending Vision Pro probably will not have. For example, the resolution of the virtual desktops is extremely low, on my 14” MacBook Pro they were around 720p each while the main display remained 1920x1080. I use an open source app called Retina DisplayMenu to adjust my screen resolution (typically higher than that for my young eagle eyes), so in some ways this doesn’t really increase my productivity (especially considering how easy it is to simply just use Spaces instead, maintaining whatever screen resolution I want). Put another way, you may be given up screen real estate for your AR experience, something I haven’t heard any reviewer really mention. That said, it’s still neat-o to experience, but I wouldn’t rush out and buy these glasses just for the spatial computing component nor would I presume these will match a forthcoming device to cost 10x more.
